title: The Great Stork Derby: When a Millionaire's Prank Turned Babies Into a Competition
summary: Imagine a contest where the winner is the mom with the most babies. Sounds crazy, right? But that's exactly what happened in Canada during the Great Depression. A wealthy Toronto lawyer and prankster named Charles Vance Millar died on Halloween in 1926. He was known for his practical jokes — like leaving money on the sidewalk just to watch people's reactions. In his will, Millar included a bizarre clause: the mother who gave birth to the most children in the 10 years after his death would win a fortune. The prize was worth about 500,000 Canadian dollars at the time — equivalent to nearly 9 million Canadian dollars today. The contest became known as the Great Stork Derby. Newspapers followed the leading contenders closely, building dramatic narratives around their lives. Two women emerged as frontrunners: Grace Bagnato, a 37-year-old Italian immigrant who had given birth to 20 children, and Florence Brown, a 42-year-old fifth-generation Canadian who had 26 kids. Brown told a reporter she didn't want "any Italian to get away with that 'leadership stuff'" and hoped more Canadians would have large families to prevent the country from being "overrun with foreigners." This comment revealed deep racial and class tensions. At the time, birth control was illegal in Canada, and the eugenics movement — a debunked ideology that argued against poor and minority families having many children — was influential. Millar's exact intentions are unclear, but some historians think he was satirizing these attitudes. The derby sparked legal battles, media frenzy, and public outrage. Some people thought the contest was immoral, while others saw it as a desperate chance to escape poverty during the Depression. In the end, four women tied and split the money. The Great Stork Derby remains one of the strangest and most controversial contests in history, revealing a lot about Canada's social values in the 1930s.
why_it_matters: This contest shows how social biases and economic desperation can shape deeply personal decisions like family size, and reminds us that wealth and power have often been used in ways that exploit vulnerable people.

keywords.explanation: A now-debunked belief that some people are better than others and should have more children.
A time in the 1930s when the economy was very bad and many people were poor.
A person who moves to a new country to live there.
The people who were most likely to win the contest at the time.
Using humor or exaggeration to criticize or make fun of something.
Causing a lot of public disagreement or strong feelings.

questions.question: Who wrote the unusual will that started the Great Stork Derby?
About how much money was the prize worth at the time of the contest?
How many children did Florence Brown have?
What law was in place in Canada at the time that affected family size?
Which movement argued that poor and minority families should not have many children?
How many women ended up splitting the prize money at the end?

background_read: Charles Vance Millar (a wealthy Toronto lawyer known for playing practical jokes) died in 1926 and left a will that included a strange contest for mothers.
The Great Depression (a severe worldwide economic downturn in the 1930s) meant many families were desperate for money, which made the prize especially tempting.

The eugenics movement (a now-discredited ideology that promoted selective breeding to 'improve' the human race) was influential in Canada and the US at the time, and often targeted immigrants and the poor.
Birth control was illegal in Canada until 1969, so women had limited control over their family size during the derby.
Article_Structure: LEAD: A bizarre baby contest in Canada during the Great Depression
└─ Hook: 'Imagine a contest where the winner is the mom with the most babies'
CONTEXT: Charles Vance Millar and his will
├─ WHO: Charles Vance Millar, a Toronto lawyer and prankster
├─ WHEN: Died Halloween 1926, contest ran 10 years
└─ WHAT: Clause giving fortune to mother with most children
└─ Florence Brown, fifth-generation Canadian, 26 children, with quote showing racial tension
DEEPER ISSUES: Birth control illegal, eugenics movement influential, historians think Millar may have been satirizing these attitudes
OUTCOME: Legal battles, media frenzy, four women tied and split the money
SIGNIFICANCE: Reveals social values of 1930s Canada

perspectives.description: The four women who split the prize money gained a financial lifeline during the Great Depression, and some families were able to improve their living conditions significantly.

The derby exploited poor women and turned childbirth into a spectacle, reinforcing racist and classist ideas. Florence Brown's public comments revealed ugly nativist attitudes that the contest encouraged.

The event reflects the social norms of 1930s Canada: birth control was illegal, the eugenics movement was mainstream, and women had little reproductive choice. The contest was both a product of its time and a mirror of its tensions.

Today, such a contest would be illegal under laws protecting children and reproductive rights. The story warns us to examine how modern incentives—like baby bonuses or surrogacy payments—might also create unintended ethical problems.
